purpose: Uses the Kalecki-Levy accounting identity to trace the dual channel through which fiscal deficits simultaneously support corporate earnings and elevate term premium, revealing the paradox that fiscal austerity may be equity-positive at current deficit levels.
components: Earnings channel: ~6.5% deficit/GDP → ~$400-600B annual flow to aggregate corporate profits → ~$15-25 of ~$250-260 S&P EPS
Term premium channel: $2T+ annual Treasury issuance → 80-130bp adjusted term premium → higher discount rates
Quality differentiation: aggregate floor does NOT protect firm-level cash flow in leveraged credits
Austerity paradox: at current deficit levels, P/E expansion from lower term premium may dominate EPS decline from reduced fiscal transfer
current_assessment: Both channels are currently active. The earnings channel supports near-term equity performance while the term premium channel builds headwinds for duration-sensitive assets. The quality differentiation means credit vulnerability is building beneath the aggregate surface. The austerity paradox remains untested but analytically sound.
limitations: EPS attribution ($15-25) involves many intermediate assumptions; plausible range is $5-40
The austerity paradox depends on specific magnitude of P/E sensitivity to term premium
Aggregate identity says nothing about distribution across sectors or quality tiers
Fiscal path dependency creates circular reasoning risks (economy resilient because of deficits, deficits sustainable because economy resilient)
